Members of the public wishing to comment on any item not appearing on the agenda may address the City Council at this time. State
law prevents Council from taking action on any matter not on the agenda; your comments may be referred to staff for follow up.
Public comment is limited to a total of 15 minutes; however, an opportunity for additional public comment may be provided later in
the agenda.
 
PUBLIC HEARING
City Council action on the following public hearing(s) represents a final decision concluding all administrative proceedings. Judicial
review may be had only if a petition is filed with the Court not later than the 90th day following the date the decision is made.
Judicial review of environmental determinations may be subject to a time period for litigation as short as 30 days following the date
the decision is made.
 
5.
668 E. 3rd Avenue, New Multi-Family Residential Development (PA-2025-025) — Approval
Adopt a Resolution to approve the Site Plan and Architectural Review (SPAR), and Site Development Planning
Application (SDPA) for construction of a new multi-family residential building with 128 units and associated site
improvements at 668 East 3rd Avenue, and find that the Project is Statutorily Exempt from the California
Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) pursuant to Assembly Bill 130, based on the findings for approval and subject
to the conditions of approval.
CEQA: In accordance with the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), the Project meets the criteria to be
statutorily exempt from further environmental review pursuant to Public Resources Code Section 21080.66. This
exemption was enacted as a part of Assembly Bill 130 (AB130), effective as of June 30, 2025. The applicant has
provided a memo detailing the Project’s qualification under AB130, included as Attachment 6. Staff have reviewed
the eligibility criteria and determined that the Project meets the qualifying criteria under AB 130.
6.
Reach Codes for the 2025 Code Cycle — Ordinance Introduction
Introduce an Ordinance to amend San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 23.24 "Energy Code" to require energy
conservation measures for air conditioner alterations, replacements and installations in single family homes,
duplexes and townhomes; energy conservation measures for heating, ventilation and air conditioner
replacements and installations in nonresidential buildings; and increased efficiency and electric readiness
requirements for additions and alterations to single family homes, duplexes, and townhomes; and adopt a
Resolution making findings that the proposed changes or modifications are reasonably necessary due to local
climatic, geographical, or topographical conditions.
CEQA: This action to adopt reach codes and amend the Energy Code is categorically exempt from CEQA as an
action by a regulatory agency for the protection of the environment, because reach codes are intended to reduce
greenhouse gas emissions. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15308.)
7.
Operation of Leaf Blowers — Ordinance Introduction
2 of 662

--- page 3 ---
Introduce an Ordinance to amend San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 10.80 "Operation of Leaf Blowers" to
prohibit the use of gas leaf blowers.
CEQA: This Ordinance prohibiting gas leaf blower operations is categorically exempt from CEQA because it
consists of an action to protect the environment by reducing greenhouse gas emissions. (Public Resources Code
Section 15308.)

provide direction to staff regarding any Council Members who wish to serve on additional Appointment
Subcommittees, whether eligible Commissioners may be automatically reappointed to an additional term, which
upcoming vacancies will require incumbents to reapply and reinterview, and any additional direction necessary to
carry out the 2026 recruitment process.
CEQA: In accordance with Public Resources Code Section 21080.66(b)(1)(A), the City formally noticed Native
American tribes for consultation regarding the Project and its potential impacts on tribal cultural resources. One
tribe responded to the City’s notice to request consultation. The Project Conditions of Approval (Attachment 1,
Exhibit A) include conditions required to satisfy requirements of AB 130 to avoid impacts to tribal cultural
resources. In addition, consistent with Public Resources Code 21080.66(c), the Project Conditions of Approval
include a requirement for the applicant to complete a phase I environmental assessment, and to mitigate any
hazards as required per the statute.

MEETING DATE:
March 02, 2026 
SUBJECT:
2026 City of San Mateo Legislative Platform — Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve the 2026 City of San Mateo Legislative Platform. 
BACKGROUND:
The City of San Mateo is considering adoption of a Legislative Platform to establish a clear, consistent framework to guide 
the City’s engagement on state legislative and budget matters. A Legislative Platform is intended to articulate the City’s 
priorities and policy positions on issues that affect municipal operations, service delivery, fiscal sustainability, and the well-
being of the San Mateo community, and to communicate those priorities to state legislators, regulatory agencies, and 
other stakeholders. 
Developing a Legislative Platform allows the City to take a more proactive and coordinated approach to legislative 
advocacy by identifying priority issue areas in advance of each legislative session. The Platform is designed to support 
informed and timely engagement on legislation, budget proposals, and funding opportunities that may impact the City, 
while preserving the City Council’s discretion to consider and adopt positions on individual legislative items as they arise. 
The Legislative Platform is intended to function as a living document that may be reviewed and updated periodically to 
reflect evolving City priorities, changes in state policy, and emerging legislative issues. Adoption of a Platform does not 
limit the City Council’s authority to direct advocacy on matters outside the Platform but rather provides an organizing 
framework to support consistent evaluation and response to state-level policy initiatives. 
DEVELOPMENT PROCESS
In preparation for the 2026 legislative session, City staff worked with the City’s legislative consultant, California Public 
Policy Group (CPPG), to develop a proposed Legislative Platform grounded in the City’s adopted policies, strategic plans, 
and operational priorities. The draft Platform was informed by a review of City guiding documents and input from City 
departments regarding key policy challenges and anticipated legislative impacts. On October 27, 2025, the draft Legislative 
Platform was reviewed by the City Council Legislative Subcommittee, whereby direction was made to make additional 
edits, and bring the draft Platform to the full City Council for consideration. 
At the February 2, 2026, Study Session, staff and CPPG presented the draft platform to the full Council and received 
direction to make edits and bring the platform back for formal adoption. Attachment 1 is the clean version of the proposed 
2026 Legislative platform with the edits from February 2 incorporated. Attachment 2 shows the edits to the Platform in 
red so the Council and public can see the proposed changes made since February 2. 
5 of 662

--- page 6 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Primarily, these changes include: 
•
Emphasizing the City’s commitment to bike and pedestrian infrastructure 
•
Adding language to affirm the City’s support of climate adaptation, sustainability, greenhouse gas reductions, and 
infrastructure resilience 
•
Adding language to affirm the City’s support of all residents, regardless of immigration status
•
Adding language to affirm the City’s support of legislation that promotes safe bicycle travel
The proposed 2026 Legislative Platform reflects a coordinated and cross-departmental process designed to align the City’s 
legislative advocacy with adopted policies, operational needs, and long-term strategic goals. As part of the development 
process, staff met with and incorporated input from multiple City departments, including:
•
City Manager’s Office 
•
Community Development 
•
Planning and Housing 
•
Public Works 
•
Parks and Recreation 
•
Economic Development and Sustainability 
•
Police Department and Homelessness Services 
•
The San Mateo Consolidated Fire Department 
CPPG and staff also reviewed key guiding documents, including the City’s Housing Element, Strategic Plan, General Plan 
2040, adopted equity and inclusion policies, and prior City Council resolutions, to ensure consistency between the 
Legislative Platform and the City’s broader policy framework. 
The Draft 2026 Legislative Platform is organized by major policy subject areas that reflect the City’s adopted priorities, 
operational responsibilities, and areas of anticipated state legislative activity. The Platform includes the following subject 
areas: 
•
Community Development and Housing 
•
Transportation and Public Works  
•
Parks, Recreation, and Community Services 
•
Governmental Transparency and Public Meeting Access 
•
Utilities, Infrastructure, and Climate Adaptation 
•
Administration and Fiscal Sustainability 
•
Public Safety and Homelessness Services 
•
Fire and Emergency Medical Services 
•
Public Safety and Homelessness Services 
Each policy section outlines guiding principles intended to preserve local authority, promote fiscal sustainability, advance 
equity and inclusivity, protect public safety, and support the City’s ability to deliver essential services to the community. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no direct fiscal impact associated with approving the Legislative Platform.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in 
direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Proposed Legislative Advocacy Policy for City of San Mateo 
SUBJECT:    Proposed State Legislative Platform 
PURPOSE 
The intent of the City of San Mateo Legislative Platform is to convey to state 
legislators, policymakers, and the public where the City stands on important 
policy issues. This Platform provides the direction needed for the City to engage 
on state initiatives that affect the City’s key priorities, programs, and/or 
operations. In line with the City’s mission, the Legislative Platform reaffirms the 
City’s commitment to serving the San Mateo community through collaboration, 
innovation, and professionalism.  
San Mateo strives to provide safe, clean, and attractive neighborhoods; 
successful businesses and solid tax base; a community where residents can 
flourish and youth are nurtured; orderly planning, development; and open, 
participative, and effective City Government. The City of San Mateo prides itself 
on being a vibrant, diverse community that is committed to equity and inclusivity, 
and stands firmly against all forms of racism, anti-immigrant sentiment, 
xenophobia and implicit bias. San Mateo is a community that is inclusive of all 
people, regardless of skin color, gender, sexual orientation or ethnicity. Respect, 
integrity, transparency and inclusivity are central values in the City, and those 
values inform the City’s legislative advocacy. 
Additionally, the City of San Mateo is a progressive community that aims to 
preserve the environment, provide economic well-being, and ensure social equity 
for our residents and businesses. Empowering local decision making and fiscal 
sustainability are underlying principles of this Platform.  
OFFICIAL POSITIONS FOR ADOPTION 
Possible positions that can be adopted on legislation include: 
Support: A support position indicates to the Legislature, regulatory 
agencies, and other stakeholders that the City is in favor of the proposed 
legislation in question. 
Sponsor: A sponsor position indicates to the Legislature, regulatory 
agencies, and other stakeholders that the City is not only in strong support 
of the legislation in question but is publicly leading the advocacy effort. 
8 of 662

--- page 9 ---
This position is only used when a Member of the Legislature has agreed to 
author a specific legislative proposal on behalf of the City.
Oppose Unless Amended: An oppose unless amended position 
indicates to the legislature, regulatory agencies, and other stakeholders 
that the City is against the proposed legislation in question, unless 
requested changes are adopted to address the City’s policy-based 
concerns. 
Oppose: An oppose position indicates to the Legislature, regulatory 
agencies, and other stakeholders that the City is against the proposed 
legislation in question. This position is often used only when/ if 
amendments or other policy compromises cannot be reached. 
Neutral: A neutral position indicates to the Legislature, regulatory 
agencies, and other stakeholders that the City has formally removed their 
prior adopted position due to changes being made to the legislation or 
proposal (e.g., a bill that the City formally adopted an “oppose unless 
amended” position  which was amended to address the City’s concerns, 
therefore the City is formally withdrawing its opposition and moving to a 
“Neutral” position). A neutral position therefore indicates that the City is 
impartial on the legislation in question.
Watch: A watch is an internal position which indicates that the City is 
closely monitoring and evaluating the legislation in question. Should 
factors change, the City reserves the right to adopt a formal external 
position.
Community Development and Housing
The City of San Mateo takes pride in its communities and works to maintain and 
improve the quality of neighborhoods and expand the City of San Mateo's 
affordable housing. Through production and preservation, utilizing public and 
private funding sources, the City has grown into a diverse and modern innovation 
hub with plentiful options for housing, employment, shopping, and leisure. 
The City is working to implement policies and programs proscribed in its certified 
housing element to achieve production goals to create a robust mix of affordable 
and market-rate housing options to house community members at all income 
levels.  
9 of 662

--- page 10 ---
San Mateo appreciates collaboration with State and regional partners to achieve 
measurable success. In that spirit, San Mateo outlines the following guiding 
policy principles for community development and housing: 
1. Support comprehensive housing legislation that addresses all contributing 
factors to California’s housing crisis.
2. Support legislation that enables the City to meet its Regional Housing 
Needs Allocation (RHNA) goals, including measures that extend 
compliance timelines, provide housing funding, and increase flexibility in 
eligible housing types—such as granting RHNA credit for each bedroom in 
multi-unit developments.
3. Support efforts to preserve existing affordable housing and protect 
housing options for extremely low-, very low-, low-, and middle-income 
residents.
4. Support incentives for housing production across all income levels—
market rate, middle, low, very low, and extremely low—while maintaining 
local land use authority.
5. Support increased local authority over the phasing of housing within 
mixed-use developments.
6. Review and evaluate legislation that would increase housing production 
ministerially, ensuring adequate opportunities for community engagement 
and City Council oversight.
7. Support legislation and funding mechanisms that promote affordable and 
regional housing projects tailored to the needs of local populations.
8. Support legislation that expands affordable homeownership opportunities 
to foster economic mobility.
9. Support funding opportunities for local jurisdictions through statewide or 
regional housing bond initiatives.
10.Oppose measures that link transportation funding to housing production 
requirements.
11.Oppose or critically evaluate legislation that directly ties production 
mandates to the RHNA process.
12.Support legislation granting additional time for the City to complete its 
housing element process.
13.Support legislation requiring the State Housing Department to provide 
clear, actionable and timely guidance for local compliance with housing 
element law. 
14.Oppose legislation that restricts or caps local development fees 
associated with housing projects at any income level.
15.Oppose legislation that impedes local authority to plan and site adequate 
parking to satisfy community needs. 
10 of 662

--- page 11 ---
16.Support legislation that increases local regulatory control on mobile and 
stationary food vending.
Transportation and Public Works 
The City of San Mateo understands the importance in ensuring residents have 
access to safe, resilient, equitable, and sustainable public infrastructure, 
including a commitment to safe bike and pedestrian infrastructure. The City 
oversees the maintenance of 210 miles of City streets, 87 signalized 
intersections, over 7,000 street lights, and 11 publicly-owned parking lots and 
garages.  
The City believes that close alignment with regional transportation partners is 
critical to meeting its goals. San Mateo outlines the following policy principles for 
transportation: 
1. Support legislation that promotes innovation in emerging technologies, 
particularly those that creatively address congestion and improve overall 
transportation system management.
2. Support legislation and funding for traffic signal synchronization initiatives 
to enhance safety at rights-of-way.
3. Support efforts to maximize SB 1 funding for local street and road 
rehabilitation projects that reduce congestion and improve safety.
4. Support legislation and funding mechanisms that advance a regional, 
multimodal, safe, and accessible transportation network.
5. Support legislation and funding that enable the City to fully implement 
designated “quiet zones.”
6. Support legislation and funding that allow the City to improve at-grade 
crossings, including the installation of quad gates as a cost-effective 
safety measure.
7. Support legislation and funding that facilitate the removal of grade 
crossings as train frequencies increase along the corridor.
8. Support proposals that enhance collaboration among the City, regional 
transportation partners, and the California High-Speed Rail Authority as 
the project advances.
9. Oppose legislation that restricts local authority over transportation 
permitting, utility relocation, or dispute resolution by imposing rigid state 
timelines or bypassing local planning, environmental review, or decision-
making.
10.Support legislation and funding for the rehabilitation and modernization of 
aging parking infrastructure.
11 of 662

--- page 12 ---
11.Support increased state funding for bicycle and pedestrian infrastructure, 
including citywide access lanes and crosswalks, and legislation that 
promotes walkable and bike-friendly communities.
12.Support legislation, programs, and funding that advance the City’s Vision 
Zero goal of eliminating traffic fatalities and severe injuries, as outlined in 
the San Mateo General Plan 2040.
13.Support regional funding initiatives that expand access to public 
transportation, including improved transit connections to community 
colleges.
14.Oppose legislation that diverts traffic onto local streets without providing 
adequate state funding to mitigate local impacts.
15.Support legislation recognizing the link between increased electric vehicle 
use and declining gas tax revenues, and evaluate potential impacts of a 
vehicle-miles-traveled (VMT) funding model.
16.Oppose legislation that redirects local transportation impact fees to a 
statewide VMT bank.
Parks, Recreation, and Community Services
The City of San Mateo’s parks and urban forests are important natural resources 
that provide community members with access to nature, encourage healthy 
lifestyles, and support a mixture of active and passive recreation opportunities. 
The City’s Park system includes more than a dozen neighborhood parks, nine 
community parks, six recreation/community centers, two pools, the Shoreline 
regional park system, several small “mini” parks, and an estuary lagoon for 
boating. Combined, the San Mateo community has access to approximately 795 
acres of parks and open space. The City hosts diverse activities from senior 
programming, dance, music, and fitness classes to youth sports and camps to 
encourage healthy community engagement.  
Creating equitable access to programs and services and collaborating with 
regional stakeholders and state legislative leaders to explore new policies to 
provide local flexibility and innovative approaches. As such, San Mateo outlines 
the following policy goals to support children, youth, and seniors: 
1. Support legislation that advances youth development, including initiatives 
that expand student enrollment, equitable access to internet and 
technology, and youth services.
2. Support legislation that promotes youth well-being and enhances school 
safety.
3. Support legislation that increases childcare funding, including resources 
for preserving existing childcare facilities and developing new ones.
12 of 662

--- page 13 ---
4. Support legislation and funding for youth scholarship programs that 
expand access to extracurricular and enrichment activities.
5. Support legislation that streamlines development processes and reduces 
barriers to improve access, affordability, and availability of childcare 
facilities.
6. Support legislation and funding opportunities to improve park facilities, 
address deferred maintenance, and encourage interagency coordination 
when appropriate.
7. Support legislation and funding that promote physical activity, including 
the maintenance and expansion of city parks, playgrounds, sports 
facilities, and community centers.
8. Support legislation and funding for youth, teen, and senior programming 
as a long-term investment in community development.
9. Support programs and funding initiatives that expand access to mental 
health services for all residents, particularly children, youth, and seniors.
10.Support legislation and funding opportunities—including federal, state, and 
conservancy grants—that strengthen communities through investments in 
clean air and water, parks, open space, natural resources, historic 
preservation, arts, and cultural amenities.
11.Oppose efforts that reduce funding for regional and community services 
that provide access to parks, open space, bike lanes, after-school 
programs, youth services, senior services, food programs, recreation 
facilities, and other essential resources supporting health, safety, and 
sustainability.
12.Support local agency authority to establish and manage policies that 
recognize and enhance the benefits of parks and recreation facilities.
13.Support state investments in urban forestry to protect and expand the tree 
canopy through long-term management plans that promote climate 
resilience, biodiversity, and equity across neighborhoods.
Governmental Transparency and Public Meeting Access  
Local agencies across the state embraced virtual/remote public meetings to 
protect the public, agency staff, and elected and appointed officials. Moreover, it 
has been observed that this approach has increased constituent participation in 
the public process while upholding the highest levels of transparency. State 
initiatives to expand public meeting access should preserve local flexibility in 
implementation and include reimbursement for any associated costs.
13 of 662

--- page 14 ---
1. Support legislation that enhances public trust, confidence, and access to 
public meetings while recognizing the fiscal and operational constraints of 
local government.
2. Support legislative and state funded efforts that strengthen public 
confidence in the electoral process through improved voter outreach, 
education, integrity measures, and sustainable resource allocation.
3. Support legislation that expands civic participation and engagement, 
including continued flexibility for subsidiary bodies to meet without 
physical posting requirements under the Ralph M. Brown Act.
4. Support legislation permitting a minority of council members to participate 
virtually without physical location posting requirements, provided an in-
person quorum is maintained.
5. Support legislation allowing councilmembers serving multi-jurisdictional 
bodies to participate remotely without physical location posting 
requirements.
6. Support state funding initiatives that enhance public access and 
participation in local government meetings and processes.
7. Oppose legislation that increases the cost of municipal hearings through 
unnecessary or burdensome new requirements.
8. Support legislation that enhances transparency and timely reporting of 
election results by establishing clear standards for official canvass 
timelines, while maintaining flexibility and funding support for local 
agencies to meet operational requirements.
9. Oppose legislation that imposes new mandates under the California Public 
Records Act without accompanying state reimbursement provisions.
10.Oppose legislation that mandates uniform, one-size-fits-all local record 
retention policies, limiting local flexibility. 
Climate Adaptation, Infrastructure, and Utilities
The City of San Mateo maintains a strong commitment to climate adaptation, 
environmental sustainability, and infrastructure resilience. As climate impacts 
intensify, including sea level rise, flooding, extreme heat, and wildfire-related 
risks, the City recognizes that local action must be supported by aligned state 
and federal policies. While San Mateo has established clear direction through its 
Climate Action Plan and related initiatives, successful implementation requires 
regulatory flexibility, technical assistance, and sustained funding partnerships. 
The City therefore seeks legislative and budgetary actions that reinforce local 
climate strategies, protect local authority, and provide meaningful support to 
advance long-term community resilience.
14 of 662

--- page 15 ---
1. Support legislation and funding that advance community resilience, 
environmental sustainability and strategies to achieve greenhouse gas 
emission reduction targets in the City's Climate Action Plan.
2. Support legislation that advances the development and long-term 
sustainability of locally operated Community Choice Aggregators (CCAs).
3. Support legislation that ensures ratepayer equity between CCAs and other 
public or private electric utilities.
4. Oppose legislation that transfers wildfire-related costs from investor-
owned utilities to cities or their residents.
5. Oppose legislation requiring streetlight and traffic signal poles to be made 
available for small wireless facility installations without local review, 
engagement, or approval.
6. Support legislation that fosters collaborative regional efforts to mitigate the 
impacts of sea level rise and flooding.
7. Support and monitor legislation that expands funding and access to water 
conservation, reuse, recycling, storage, and other water supply 
technologies.
8. Support legislation that strengthens the City’s ability to maintain and 
enhance local utilities and infrastructure.
9. Advocate for increased funding for stormwater management programs 
and the ongoing maintenance of stormwater infrastructure.
10.Oppose legislation that restructures stormwater quality programs by 
imposing new penalties or unfunded mandates that increase local cost 
burdens. 
Other policy-related matters important to the City of San Mateo Include:  
Welcoming Community 
1. Support legislation that aligns with the City’s commitment to fostering a 
community where everyone can thrive regardless of background. This 
includes supporting the fundamental principles of the City of San Mateo’s 
Resolution No. 24 (2017) and the protection of the City’s residents, 
regardless of immigration status.
Solid Waste and Recycling 
1. Support legislation that preserves and strengthens local authority and 
fiscal flexibility to regulate solid waste and recycling operations.
2. Support adequate state funding for new solid waste and recycling 
requirements to offset associated program costs.
3. Support legislation and funding opportunities that promote the 
development of local and regional recycling and composting facilities. 
15 of 662

--- page 16 ---
Administration and Fiscal Sustainability  
1. Support legislation and funding initiatives that assist local governments in 
stabilizing and managing increasing pension obligations, including through 
the use of excess State budget surplus revenues.
2. Support legislation that advances San Mateo County’s efforts to revise 
statutes related to the Vehicle Licensing Fee (VLF) shortfall to ensure the 
appropriate allocation of excess Educational Revenue Augmentation Fund 
(ERAF) monies.
3. Support legislation and ballot measures that protect the City’s fiscal 
health, responsibilities, legal authority, and operations; uphold existing 
City policies, prior actions, or current City Council strategic priorities; and 
enhance programs, services, or infrastructure that benefit San Mateo 
residents.
4. Oppose legislation and ballot measures that threaten the City’s financial 
stability, responsibilities, legal authority, or operations; conflict with 
existing City policies, prior actions, or current City Council strategic 
priorities; or diminish programs, services, or infrastructure serving the San 
Mateo community.
5. Support legislation and ballot measures that provide additional or 
enhanced funding mechanisms to construct, reconstruct, rehabilitate, or 
replace public infrastructure; develop affordable and permanent supportive 
housing; or acquire or lease property for those purposes.
6. Oppose legislation that restricts the City’s authority to contract for 
professional services or imposes unnecessary and costly delays in the 
contracting process.
Public Safety and Homelessness Services 
2. Support legislation, ballot measures, and funding mechanisms that

responders.

times resulting from increased density, traffic calming measures, and

when appropriate.
5. Support legislation that strengthens local law enforcement capacity and 
promotes regional collaboration and data-sharing in resource deployment 
and specialized task force operations.
6. Support state funding for local law enforcement to enforce the provisions 
enacted in Proposition 36.

Proposed Legislative Advocacy Policy for City of San Mateo  
 
SUBJECT:    Proposed State Legislative Platform  
 
PURPOSE  
The intent of the City of San Mateo Legislative Platform is to convey to state 
legislators, policymakers, and the public where the City stands on important policy 
issues. This Platform provides the direction needed for the City to engage on state 
initiatives that affect the City’s key priorities, programs, and/or operations. In line 
with the City’s mission, the Legislative Platform reaffirms the City’s commitment to 
serving the San Mateo community through collaboration, innovation, and 
professionalism.   
San Mateo strives to provide safe, clean, and attractive neighborhoods; successful 
businesses and solid tax base; a community where residents can flourish and youth 
are nurtured; orderly planning, development; and open, participative, and effective 
City Government. The City of San Mateo prides itself on being a vibrant, diverse 
community that is committed to equity and inclusivity, and stands firmly against all 
forms of racism, anti-immigrant sentiment, xenophobia and implicit bias. San 
Mateo is a community that is inclusive of all people, regardless of skin color, 
gender, sexual orientation or ethnicity. Respect, integrity, transparency and 
inclusivity are central values in the City, and those values inform the City’s 
legislative advocacy.  
Additionally, the City of San Mateo is a progressive community that aims to preserve 
the environment, provide economic well-being, and ensure social equity for our 
residents and businesses. Empowering local decision making and fiscal 
sustainability are underlying principles of this Platform.   
 
OFFICIAL POSITIONS FOR ADOPTION  
Possible positions that can be adopted on legislation include:  
 
Support: A support position indicates to the Legislature, regulatory 
agencies, and other stakeholders that the City is in favor of the proposed 
legislation in question.  
 
19 of 662

--- page 20 ---
Sponsor: A sponsor position indicates to the Legislature, regulatory 
agencies, and other stakeholders that the City is not only in strong support of 
the legislation in question but is publicly leading the advocacy effort. This 
position is only used when a Member of the Legislature has agreed to author 
a specific legislative proposal on behalf of the City. 
 
Oppose Unless Amended: An oppose unless amended position indicates to 
the legislature, regulatory agencies, and other stakeholders that the City is 
against the proposed legislation in question, unless requested changes are 
adopted to address the City’s policy-based concerns.  
 
Oppose: An oppose position indicates to the Legislature, regulatory 
agencies, and other stakeholders that the City is against the proposed 
legislation in question. This position is often used only when/ if amendments 
or other policy compromises cannot be reached.  
 
Neutral: A neutral position indicates to the Legislature, regulatory agencies, 
and other stakeholders that the City has formally removed their prior 
adopted position due to changes being made to the legislation or proposal 
(e.g., a bill that the City formally adopted an “oppose unless amended” 
position  which was amended to address the City’s concerns, therefore the 
City is formally withdrawing its opposition and moving to a “Neutral” 
position). A neutral position therefore indicates that the City is impartial on 
the legislation in question. 
 
Watch: A watch is an internal position which indicates that the City is 
closely monitoring and evaluating the legislation in question. Should factors 
change, the City reserves the right to adopt a formal external position. 
 
Community Development and Housing 
The City of San Mateo takes pride in its communities and works to maintain and 
improve the quality of neighborhoods and expand the City of San Mateo's affordable 
housing. Through production and preservation, utilizing public and private funding 
sources, the City has grown into a diverse and modern innovation hub with plentiful 
options for housing, employment, shopping, and leisure.  
The City is working to implement policies and programs proscribed in its certified 
housing element to achieve production goals to create a robust mix of affordable 
20 of 662

--- page 21 ---
and market-rate housing options to house community members at all income 
levels.   
San Mateo appreciates collaboration with State and regional partners to achieve 
measurable success. In that spirit, San Mateo outlines the following guiding policy 
principles for community development and housing:  
1. Support comprehensive housing legislation that addresses all contributing 
factors to California’s housing crisis. 
2. Support legislation that enables the City to meet its Regional Housing Needs 
Allocation (RHNA) goals, including measures that extend compliance 
timelines, provide housing funding, and increase flexibility in eligible housing 
types—such as granting RHNA credit for each bedroom in multi-unit 
developments. 
3. Support efforts to preserve existing affordable housing and protect housing 
options for extremely low-, very low-, low-, and middle-income residents. 
4. Support incentives for housing production across all income levels—market 
rate, middle, low, very low, and extremely low—while maintaining local land 
use authority. 
5. Support increased local authority over the phasing of housing within mixed-
use developments. 
6. Review and evaluate legislation that would increase housing production 
ministerially, ensuring adequate opportunities for community engagement 
and City Council oversight. 
7. Support legislation and funding mechanisms that promote affordable and 
regional housing projects tailored to the needs of local populations. 
8. Support legislation that expands affordable homeownership opportunities to 
foster economic mobility. 
9. Support funding opportunities for local jurisdictions through statewide or 
regional housing bond initiatives. 
10. Oppose measures that link transportation funding to housing production 
requirements. 
11. Oppose or critically evaluate legislation that directly ties production 
mandates to the RHNA process. 
12. Support legislation granting additional time for the City to complete its 
housing element process. 
13. Support legislation requiring the State Housing Department to provide clear, 
actionable and timely guidance for local compliance with housing element 
law.  
21 of 662

--- page 22 ---
14. Oppose legislation that restricts or caps local development fees associated 
with housing projects at any income level. 
15. Oppose legislation that impedes local authority to plan and site adequate 
parking to satisfy community needs.  
16. Support legislation that increases local regulatory control on mobile and 
stationary food vending. 
 
Transportation and Public Works  
The City of San Mateo understands the importance in ensuring residents have 
access to safe, resilient, equitable, and sustainable public infrastructure, including 
a commitment to safe bike and pedestrian infrastructure. The City oversees the 
maintenance of 210 miles of City streets, 87 signalized intersections, over 7,000 
street lights, and 11 publicly-owned parking lots and garages.   
The City believes that close alignment with regional transportation partners is 
critical to meeting its goals. San Mateo outlines the following policy principles for 
transportation:  
1. Support legislation that promotes innovation in emerging technologies, 
particularly those that creatively address congestion and improve overall 
transportation system management. 
2. Support legislation and funding for traffic signal synchronization initiatives to 
enhance safety at rights-of-way. 
3. Support efforts to maximize SB 1 funding for local street and road 
rehabilitation projects that reduce congestion and improve safety. 
4. Support legislation and funding mechanisms that advance a regional, 
multimodal, safe, and accessible transportation network. 
5. Support legislation and funding that enable the City to fully implement 
designated “quiet zones.” 
6. Support legislation and funding that allow the City to improve at-grade 
crossings, including the installation of quad gates as a cost-effective safety 
measure. 
7. Support legislation and funding that facilitate the removal of grade crossings 
as train frequencies increase along the corridor. 
8. Support proposals that enhance collaboration among the City, regional 
transportation partners, and the California High-Speed Rail Authority as the 
project advances. 
22 of 662

--- page 23 ---
9. Oppose legislation that restricts local authority over transportation 
permitting, utility relocation, or dispute resolution by imposing rigid state 
timelines or bypassing local planning, environmental review, or decision-
making. 
10. Support legislation and funding for the rehabilitation and modernization of 
aging parking infrastructure. 
11. Support increased state funding for bicycle and pedestrian infrastructure, 
including citywide access lanes and crosswalks, and legislation that 
promotes walkable and bike-friendly communities. 
12. Support legislation, programs, and funding that advance the City’s Vision 
Zero goal of eliminating traffic fatalities and severe injuries, as outlined in the 
San Mateo General Plan 2040. 
13. Support regional funding initiatives that expand access to public 
transportation, including improved transit connections to community 
colleges. 
14. Oppose legislation that diverts traffic onto local streets without providing 
adequate state funding to mitigate local impacts. 
15. Support legislation recognizing the link between increased electric vehicle 
use and declining gas tax revenues, and evaluate potential impacts of a 
vehicle-miles-traveled (VMT) funding model. 
16. Oppose legislation that redirects local transportation impact fees to a 
statewide VMT bank. 
 
Parks, Recreation, and Community Services 
The City of San Mateo’s parks and urban forests are important natural resources 
that provide community members with access to nature, encourage healthy 
lifestyles, and support a mixture of active and passive recreation opportunities. The 
City’s Park system includes more than a dozen neighborhood parks, nine 
community parks, six recreation/community centers, two pools, the Shoreline 
regional park system, several small “mini” parks, and an estuary lagoon for boating. 
Combined, the San Mateo community has access to approximately 795 acres of 
parks and open space. The City hosts diverse activities from senior programming, 
dance, music, and fitness classes to youth sports and camps to encourage healthy 
community engagement.   
Creating equitable access to programs and services and collaborating with regional 
stakeholders and state legislative leaders to explore new policies to provide local 
23 of 662

--- page 24 ---
flexibility and innovative approaches. As such, San Mateo outlines the following 
policy goals to support children, youth, and seniors:  
1. Support legislation that advances youth development, including initiatives 
that expand student enrollment, equitable access to internet and 
technology, and youth services. 
2. Support legislation that promotes youth well-being and enhances school 
safety. 
3. Support legislation that increases childcare funding, including resources for 
preserving existing childcare facilities and developing new ones. 
4. Support legislation and funding for youth scholarship programs that expand 
access to extracurricular and enrichment activities. 
5. Support legislation that streamlines development processes and reduces 
barriers to improve access, affordability, and availability of childcare 
facilities. 
6. Support legislation and funding opportunities to improve park facilities, 
address deferred maintenance, and encourage interagency coordination 
when appropriate. 
7. Support legislation and funding that promote physical activity, including the 
maintenance and expansion of city parks, playgrounds, sports facilities, and 
community centers. 
8. Support legislation and funding for youth, teen, and senior programming as a 
long-term investment in community development. 
9. Support programs and funding initiatives that expand access to mental 
health services for all residents, particularly children, youth, and seniors. 
10. Support legislation and funding opportunities—including federal, state, and 
conservancy grants—that strengthen communities through investments in 
clean air and water, parks, open space, natural resources, historic 
preservation, arts, and cultural amenities. 
11. Oppose efforts that reduce funding for regional and community services that 
provide access to parks, open space, bike lanes, after-school programs, 
youth services, senior services, food programs, recreation facilities, and 
other essential resources supporting health, safety, and sustainability. 
12. Support local agency authority to establish and manage policies that 
recognize and enhance the benefits of parks and recreation facilities. 
13. Support state investments in urban forestry to protect and expand the tree 
canopy through long-term management plans that promote climate 
resilience, biodiversity, and equity across neighborhoods. 
24 of 662

--- page 25 ---
Governmental Transparency and Public Meeting Access   
Local agencies across the state embraced virtual/remote public meetings to 
protect the public, agency staff, and elected and appointed officials. Moreover, it 
has been observed that this approach has increased constituent participation in the 
public process while upholding the highest levels of transparency. State initiatives 
to expand public meeting access should preserve local flexibility in implementation 
and include reimbursement for any associated costs. 
1. Support legislation that enhances public trust, confidence, and access to 
public meetings while recognizing the fiscal and operational constraints of 
local government. 
2. Support legislative and state funded efforts that strengthen public 
confidence in the electoral process through improved voter outreach, 
education, integrity measures, and sustainable resource allocation. 
3. Support legislation that expands civic participation and engagement, 
including continued flexibility for subsidiary bodies to meet without physical 
posting requirements under the Ralph M. Brown Act. 
4. Support legislation permitting a minority of council members to participate 
virtually without physical location posting requirements, provided an in-
person quorum is maintained. 
5. Support legislation allowing councilmembers serving multi-jurisdictional 
bodies to participate remotely without physical location posting 
requirements. 
6. Support state funding initiatives that enhance public access and 
participation in local government meetings and processes. 
7. Oppose legislation that increases the cost of municipal hearings through 
unnecessary or burdensome new requirements. 
8. Support legislation that enhances transparency and timely reporting of 
election results by establishing clear standards for official canvass 
timelines, while maintaining flexibility and funding support for local agencies 
to meet operational requirements. 
9. Oppose legislation that imposes new mandates under the California Public 
Records Act without accompanying state reimbursement provisions. 
10. Oppose legislation that mandates uniform, one-size-fits-all local record 
retention policies, limiting local flexibility.  
 
25 of 662

--- page 26 ---
Climate Adaptation, Infrastructure, and Utilities 
The City of San Mateo maintains a strong commitment to climate adaptation, 
environmental sustainability, and infrastructure resilience. As climate impacts 
intensify, including sea level rise, flooding, extreme heat, and wildfire-related 
risks, the City recognizes that local action must be supported by aligned state 
and federal policies. While San Mateo has established clear direction through 
its Climate Action Plan and related initiatives, successful implementation 
requires regulatory flexibility, technical assistance, and sustained funding 
partnerships. The City therefore seeks legislative and budgetary actions that 
reinforce local climate strategies, protect local authority, and provide 
meaningful support to advance long-term community resilience. 
1. Support legislation and funding that advance community resilience, 
environmental sustainability and strategies to achieve greenhouse gas 
emission reduction targets in the City's Climate Action Plan. 
2. Support legislation that advances the development and long-term 
sustainability of locally operated Community Choice Aggregators (CCAs). 
3. Support legislation that ensures ratepayer equity between CCAs and other 
public or private electric utilities. 
4. Oppose legislation that transfers wildfire-related costs from investor-owned 
utilities to cities or their residents. 
5. Oppose legislation requiring streetlight and traffic signal poles to be made 
available for small wireless facility installations without local review, 
engagement, or approval. 
6. Support legislation that fosters collaborative regional efforts to mitigate the 
impacts of sea level rise and flooding. 
7. Support and monitor legislation that expands funding and access to water 
conservation, reuse, recycling, storage, and other water supply 
technologies. 
8. Support legislation that strengthens the City’s ability to maintain and 
enhance local utilities and infrastructure. 
9. Advocate for increased funding for stormwater management programs and 
the ongoing maintenance of stormwater infrastructure. 
10. Oppose legislation that restructures stormwater quality programs by 
imposing new penalties or unfunded mandates that increase local cost 
burdens.

City policies, prior actions, or current City Council strategic priorities; or 
diminish programs, services, or infrastructure serving the San Mateo 
community.

Agenda Number:  3
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 10-3133
File ID: 26-10911
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Human Resources Department
MEETING DATE:
March 02, 2026 
SUBJECT:
Reclassification of Administrative Technician position to Housing Specialist II position — Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to re-classify one Administrative Technician position in the Community Development Department 
(CDD), Housing Division to a Housing Specialist II position. 
BACKGROUND:
The CDD Housing Division has four permanent positions, including one Housing and Neighborhood Services Manager, two 
Housing Specialists, and one Administrative Technician. Currently, the Administrative Technician position is vacant. 
The Administrative Technician position has historically performed highly specialized tasks related to administration of the 
City’s Below Market-Rate (BMR) program along with other responsibilities that support the City’s housing programs. This is 
a public-facing position that requires a high level of customer service and responsibility for overseeing the City’s portfolio 
of 470 BMR units, including 334 rental units and 136 for-sale units, in addition to monitoring 1,300 deed-restricted 
affordable rental housing developed and managed by nonprofit affordable housing organizations. This requires knowledge 
of the City’s BMR policies, loan management, real estate transactions, and affordable housing regulations. 
Given the specialized knowledge required and the level of responsibility associated with these duties, the Housing 
Specialist II classification better reflects the required duties and level of responsibilities to meet operational needs. The 
Housing Specialist II is the journey-level class in the Housing Specialist series and independently performs a variety of 
duties related to housing program implementation, grant and loan administration, and other housing and neighborhood 
service policy related work. 
To more accurately align the classification with the work performed, the CDD and Human Resources Department are 
requesting to reclassify the  position from Administrative Technician to Housing Specialist II. Reclassifying the position 
from Administrative Technician to Housing Specialist II will aid in recruitment of qualified candidates to fill this vacant 
position and provide technical support needed by the CDD Housing Division. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
In the current fiscal year 25-26 CDD personnel budget, the Administrative Technician position is funded 85% from the 
General Fund (F100), 5% from the Low/Moderate Income Housing Asset Fund (F255), 5% from the Advanced Planning 
Fund (F225), and 5% from Construction Services Fund (F230). 
It should be noted that the personnel budget for Housing Division positions, including the Administrative Technician, does 
not reflect the true funding distribution of each position. For example, the City’s share of HOME program administration 
funds from its participation in the HOME Consortium of San Mateo County (approximately $10,000 annually) offsets the 
30 of 662

--- page 31 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
salary costs of the Administrative Technician position, as does a portion of the administration budget of the Community 
Development Block Grant (CDBG). These adjustments are made at the end of each fiscal year by the Finance Department. 
The resulting distribution of costs is therefore less than 85% of General Fund each year. 
The fully burdened annual cost of a Housing Specialist II is approximately $197,000 at Step 5, compared to approximately 
$177,000 at Step 5 for an Administrative Technician. Reclassification of this position from Administrative Technician to a 
Housing Specialist II would therefore result in an approximate $20,000 annual increase. Shifting from an Administrative 
Technician to the Housing Specialist classification allows for a greater percentage of restricted Housing funds, in particular 
the Low-Moderate Income Housing Asset Fund, to cover the cost of the position. Staff is currently in the process of shifting 
more of the cost distribution for all Housing Division positions into restricted housing funds which will reduce the burden 
on the General Fund in future years. 
The monthly salary range for the Housing Specialist II position is $9,125.49 - $10,877.98. This compares to a monthly salary 
of $7,930.53 - $9,455.33 for an Administrative Technician position. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This reclassification is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that there is no possibility that it 
would have a significant effect on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(3).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Proposed Resolution

Yuliya Shukhat, Senior Risk Management Analyst

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. __ (2026)
RECLASSIFICATION OF ADMINISTRATIVE TECHNICIAN POSITION TO HOUSING SPECIALIST II POSITION
WHEREAS, pursuant to the San Mateo Municipal Code Section 2.57.030(d), approval of new 
classifications and related job specifications, salary schedules, compensation changes, elimination or 
reinstatement of positions, reallocation of positions, reclassifications, and revisions of job specifications must be 
approved by City Council Resolution; and
WHEREAS, the proposed changes as detailed below are in response to Community Development 
Department’s request to re-classify a vacant Administrative Technician position to a Housing Specialist II position 
within the Community Development Department; and
WHEREAS, the current position requires specialized program knowledge and independent judgment that 
more closely align with the duties and requirements of the Housing Specialist II classification; and
WHEREAS, the employee association representing the impacted classification has been informed of the 
proposed change and has not brought forth any objections or concerns.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES
that:
1.
This reclassification is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that there 
is no possibility that it would have a significant effect on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15378(b)(3).)
2.
A vacant Administrative Technician position in the Housing Division is re-classified to a Housing 
Specialist II position.

Section Name: PUBLIC HEARING
Account Number: 26-3111

TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
March 02, 2026 
SUBJECT:
668 E. 3rd Avenue, New Multi-Family Residential Development (PA-2025-025) — Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve the Site Plan and Architectural Review (SPAR), and Site Development Planning Application 
(SDPA) for construction of a new multi-family residential building with 128 units and associated site improvements at 668 
East 3rd Avenue, and find that the Project is Statutorily Exempt from the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) 
pursuant to Assembly Bill 130, based on the findings for approval and subject to the conditions of approval. 
BACKGROUND:
On April 2, 2025, Prometheus Real Estate Group (applicant) submitted a Planning Application (PA-2025-025) to demolish the 
existing one-story commercial shopping center known as Gateway Crossing (13,650 square feet) and construct a new 
multifamily residential building (“Project”). The proposed residential building will be eight stories tall, with one level of below 
grade parking (73 spaces), be approximately 169,750 square feet in size and include 128 residential units. Of the 128 
residential units, 20 are proposed to be provided as below market rate (BMR) affordable housing units (10 at the very low-
income affordability level and 10 at the moderate-income affordability level). The proposed unit mix includes studio, one 
bedroom, two-bedroom and three-bedroom units. This application follows the submittal of a Preliminary Application 
(PA2024-065) made under the Housing Crisis Act (Senate Bill 330) which was submitted on November 13, 2024. A 
Neighborhood Informational Meeting was held by the applicant on March 19, 2025.
On February 10, 2026, the Planning Commission held a public hearing to consider the Project. Following public comments 
and deliberation, the Planning Commission voted unanimously (4-0 with one commissioner recused) to recommend that the 
City Council approve the project. The Commission included a recommendation to provide flexibility for the applicant to 
propose a more colorful decorative tile at the project entry feature.  The Council may wish to consider this recommendation 
and update the project conditions accordingly as part of their action.  The applicant noted that they are supportive of having 
additional flexibility regarding the tile color.
This project requires approval by the City Council in accordance with SMMC Section 27.06.050(d), as the Project exceeds the 
height limit in the General Plan. The Project site has a General Plan Land Use Designation of Mixed-Use Medium II, which 
allows buildings up to six stories. The proposed Project has a height of eight stories and is utilizing a State Density Bonus 
waiver to exceed this General Plan standard, discussed in further detail below. 
Project Site
The Project site is located at 668 East 3rd Avenue, bounded by South Eldorado Street on the east, East 3rd Avenue on the 
north, and South Delaware Street on the west. The site is 0.64 acres (28,387 square feet) in size, has a General Plan Land 
Use designation of Mixed-Use Medium II, and is zoned CBD/S (Central Business District Support). The site is also within the 
Downtown Area Plan Gateway Sub-Area. The existing site is developed with a one-story 13,650 square-foot commercial 
36 of 662

--- page 37 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
building with surface parking. Adjacent uses include a gas station and office/commercial buildings on the same block just 
south of the Project site, the four-story Metropolitan Apartments complex across South Eldorado Street to the east, a 
currently vacant lot with an approved entitlement for a six-story mixed-use building across South Delaware Street (known 
as “Block 21”) to the west, and a two-story commercial building and gas station/car wash across East 3rd Avenue to the 
north. The downtown San Mateo Caltrain stop is approximately 0.3 miles walking distance from the site to the northwest. A 
location map showing the Project site is included as Attachment 2.
Project Description
The Project includes an eight-story multi-family residential building with 128 residential units and 73 off-street covered 
parking stalls. Of the total 128 units, 20 units are provided as Below Market Rate (BMR) affordable units (10 of which will be 
provided at the very-low income affordability level, and the other 10 will be at the moderate-income affordability level). The 
unit mix is shown in Table 1 below. The BMR units are distributed throughout each story of the building.
Table 1  - Proposed Units
