--- page 591 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
DRAFT ORDINANCE
Amending San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 10.80 “Operation of Leaf Blowers.”
WHEREAS, gas-powered leaf blowers emit greenhouse gases and other pollutants including carbon 
monoxide, nitrogen oxides and hydrocarbons that are harmful to human health and the environment; and
WHEREAS, research by the California Air Resources Board has identified the use of gas-powered small 
off-road engines, including leaf blowers, as detrimental to the environment as they emit high levels of air 
pollutants like nitrogen oxides and other reactive organic gases; and
WHEREAS, modern, readily available electric leaf blower equipment generally produces less noise and 
air pollution; and
WHEREAS, in 1997, the City Council adopted a leaf blower ordinance to regulate leaf blower use and 
establish hours of operation; and
WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo Climate Action Plan seeks to reduce greenhouse gas emissions 40% 
below 1990 levels by 2030 and 85% below 1990 levels and achieve carbon neutrality by 2045; and
WHEREAS, the City Council seeks to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and air pollution by prohibiting 
the use of gas leaf blowers in San Mateo and modify hours of operation of leaf blower use. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO ORDAINS AS FOLLOWS:
Section 1. Section 10.80.010 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows:
10.80.010 Purpose.
It has been found that leaf blowers cause considerable noise and air pollution and have been the source 
of numerous complaints by San Mateo residents. This chapter is intended to regulate the use of leaf blowers to 
minimize noise and air pollution in the City.
Section 2. Section 10.80.020 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows:
10.80.020 Leaf Blower – Definition.
“Electric leaf blower” is defined as any leaf blower powered by electric means, including but not limited 
to, battery-powered equipment.
“Gas leaf blower” is defined as any leaf blower powered by a combustion engine using gasoline or any 
other liquid or gaseous fluid.
“Leaf blowers” are defined as portable equipment that is powered by a self-contained  engine and used 
in any landscape, maintenance, construction, property repair, or property maintenance for the purpose of 
591 of 662

--- page 592 ---
blowing, dispersing or redistributing dust, dirt, leaves, grass, clippings, cuttings and trimmings from trees, shrubs 
or other debris.
Section 3. Section 10.80.025 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is added as follows:
10.80.025 Prohibition of Gas Leaf Blowers.
It shall be unlawful for any person to operate or authorize the operation of a gas leaf blower within the 
city limits.
Section 4. Section 10.80.030 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows:
10.80.030 Restriction of Electric Leaf Blowers - Hours of Operation.
OPTION 1:
No person shall use or operate an electric leaf blower in a residential zoning district or in areas 
bordering a residential zoning district before 8:00 a.m. or after 5:00 p.m. Monday through Friday, nor prior to 
9:00 a.m. or after 5:00 p.m. on Saturdays. Use of electric leaf blowers on Sundays or on the following holidays is 
prohibited: New Years Day, Martin Luther King, Jr. Birthday, Presidents' Day, Memorial Day, 4th of July, Labor 
Day, Veterans Day, Thanksgiving, Christmas.
OPTION 2:
No person shall use or operate an electric leaf blower before 8:00 a.m. or after 5:00 p.m. Monday 
through Friday, nor prior to 9:00 a.m. or after 5:00 p.m. on Saturdays. Use of electric leaf blowers on Sundays or 
on the following holidays is prohibited: New Years Day, Martin Luther King, Jr. Birthday, Presidents' Day, 
Memorial Day, 4th of July, Labor Day, Veterans Day, Thanksgiving, Christmas.
OPTION 3:
(a)  Except as provided in subsection (b), no person shall use or operate an electric leaf blower before 
8:00 a.m. or after 5:00 p.m. Monday through Friday, nor prior to 9:00 a.m. or after 5:00 p.m. on Saturdays. 
(b) No person shall use or operate an electric leaf blower on properties with a commercial, office or 
industrial use, or on City-owned property, before 7:00 am or after 6:00 pm Monday through Friday, nor prior to 
8:00 am or after 6:00 pm on Saturdays. 
(c) Use of electric leaf blowers on Sundays or on the following holidays is prohibited: New Years Day, 
Martin Luther King, Jr. Birthday, Presidents' Day, Memorial Day, 4th of July, Labor Day, Veterans Day, 
Thanksgiving, Christmas.
Section 5. Section 10.80.060 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows:
592 of 662

--- page 593 ---
10.80.060 Violations.
It is unlawful for any person or entity to violate the provisions of this chapter. Enforcement of this 
chapter shall be subject to enforcement pursuant to Title 1 of the San Mateo Municipal Code or any legal 
remedy available by law. Violations of this chapter may be enforced against the leaf blower operator and/or 
property owner who has hired, employed or engaged the services of a person or business utilizing leaf blowers. 
Section 6. Environmental Determination. In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) 
Guidelines Section 15308, this action is categorically exempt from CEQA, because it consists of an action to 
protect the environment by reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Section 7. Severability. In the event any section, clause or provision of this ordinance shall be determined 
invalid or unconstitutional, such section, clause or provision shall be deemed severable and all other sections or 
portions hereof shall remain in full force and effect.
Section 8. Publication. This Ordinance shall be published in summary in a newspaper of general circulation, 
posted in the City Clerk's Office, and posted on the City's website, all in accord with Section 2.15 of the City 
Charter.
Section 9. Legislative History and Effective Date. This ordinance was introduced on Clerk to complete., and 
adopted on Clerk to complete., and shall be effective on January 1, 2027.
593 of 662

--- page 594 ---
Chapter 10.80
OPERATION OF LEAF BLOWERS
10.80.010 PURPOSE.
It has been found that leaf blowers cause considerable noise and air pollution 
and have been the source of numerous complaints by San Mateo residentscitizens 
living in residential zoning districts. This chapter is intended to regulate the use of leaf 
blowers to minimize noise and air pollution in the City's residentially zoned 
neighborhoods and in areas bordering residentially zoned neighborhoods.
10.80.020 LEAF BLOWER—DEFINITION.
“Electric leaf blower” is defined as any leaf blower powered by electric means, 
including but not limited to, battery-powered equipment.
“Gas leaf blower” is defined as any leaf blower powered by a combustion engine 
using gasoline or any other liquid or gaseous fluid.
“Leaf blowers” are defined as portable equipment that is powered by a self-
contained fuel engine and used in any landscape, maintenance, construction, property 
repair, or property maintenance for the purpose of blowing, dispersing or redistributing 
dust, dirt, leaves, grass, clippings, cuttings and trimmings from trees, shrubs or other 
debris.
10.80.025 PROHIBITION OF GAS LEAF BLOWERS.
It shall be unlawful for any person to operate or authorize the operation of a gas 
leaf blower within the city limits.
10.80.030 RESTRICTION OF ELECTRIC LEAF BLOWERS - HOURS OF OPERATION.
OPTION 1:
No person shall use or operate aan electric leaf blower in a residential zoning 
district or in areas bordering a residential zoning district before 8:00 a.m. or after 5:00 
p.m. Monday through Friday, nor prior to 9:00 a.m. or after 5:00 p.m. on Saturdays. Use 
of electric leaf blowers on Sundays or on the following holidays is prohibited: New Years 
Day, Martin Luther King, Jr. Birthday, Presidents' Day, Memorial Day, 4th of July, Labor 
Day, Veterans Day, Thanksgiving, Christmas.
OPTION 2:
594 of 662

--- page 595 ---
No person shall use or operate an electric leaf blower before 8:00 a.m. or after 
5:00 p.m. Monday through Friday, nor prior to 9:00 a.m. or after 5:00 p.m. on Saturdays. 
Use of electric leaf blowers on Sundays or on the following holidays is prohibited: New 
Years Day, Martin Luther King, Jr. Birthday, Presidents' Day, Memorial Day, 4th of July, 
Labor Day, Veterans Day, Thanksgiving, Christmas.
OPTION 3:
(a)  Except as provided in subsection (b), no person shall use or operate an 
electric leaf blower before 8:00 a.m. or after 5:00 p.m. Monday through Friday, nor prior 
to 9:00 a.m. or after 5:00 p.m. on Saturdays. 
(b) No person shall use or operate an electric leaf blower on properties with a 
commercial, office or industrial use, or on City-owned property, before 7:00 am or after 
6:00 pm Monday through Friday, nor prior to 8:00 am or after 6:00 pm on Saturdays. 
(c) Use of electric leaf blowers on Sundays or on the following holidays is 
prohibited: New Years Day, Martin Luther King, Jr. Birthday, Presidents' Day, Memorial 
Day, 4th of July, Labor Day, Veterans Day, Thanksgiving, Christmas.
10.80.040
LIMITATIONS ON USE.
Any person who uses or operates a leaf blower in a residential zoning district or 
in areas bordering a residential zoning district shall use a blower nozzle extension and 
shall run the blower at a low speed to minimize noise. No more than one blower shall be 
operated at a residence at any time.
10.80.050 REMOVAL OF DEBRIS.
No material or matter blown by leaf blowers shall be blown upon neighboring 
properties or onto any portion of the public right-of-way, including but not limited to 
sidewalks and streets, without being immediately removed.
10.80.060 VIOLATIONS.
It is unlawful for any person or entity to violate the provisions of this chapter. 
Enforcement of this chapter shall be subject to enforcement pursuant to Title 1 of the 
San Mateo Municipal Code or any legal remedy available by law. Violations of this 
chapter may be enforced against the leaf blower operator and/or property owner who 
has hired, employed or engaged the services of a person or business utilizing leaf 
blowers. 
595 of 662

--- page 596 ---
10.80.070 CONSTRUCTION.
No section of this chapter shall impose a mandatory duty on the City, or on any 
officer, official, agent, employee, board, council, or commission of the City. Instead, if 
any section purports to impose a mandatory duty of enforcement, that section shall be 
deemed to invest the City, and the appropriate officer, official, agent, employee, board, 
council, or commission with discretion to enforce the section or not to enforce it.

Fw: Ban leaf blowers!!
Date:
Saturday, January 24, 2026 9:00:16 AM

From: Susan Shankle <

To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>
Cc: Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>
Subject: Ban leaf blowers!!
 
RE: City ordinance to prohibit the use of gas-powered leaf blowers to help
reduce greenhouse gas emissions and improve local air quality. 
Hello,
 
I agree with all the proposals submitted over the past decade to ban leaf blowers. Why
do we have to put up with these things?
 
Gas leaf blowers were banned in CA in 2024, but they are still being used. And the
electric ones are still a problem: they blow dust, dirt, pesticides and allergens into the
air we breathe, and create terrible noise pollution. They generate between 65-100
decibels. 
 
Instead of chasing those leaves around the street in the wind, workers should use
brooms or rakes. No big deal. 
 
At one point, it was suggested that at least the frequency of use could be regulated, like
only at certain times or certain days of the week. But they can interrupt a conversation or
a peaceful moment at any time of the day or week. 
 
Just ban them, and be done with it. 
 
Thank you,
   Susan Shankle
   33-year San Mateo resident

Leaf blowers and Reach code

Dear members of the San Mateo City Council,
I would like to urge members to support what has been done in Burlingame, Hillsborough,
Menlo Park, and Pall Alto; and which was also recommended by the San Mateo Sustainability
and Infrastructure Commission at its meeting of September 10, 2025:
1) Place a limit on hours of leaf blower operation in residential areas and adjacent commercial
areas.
2) Begin enforcement of the new leaf blower Ordinance nine months after adoption, and
3) Recommend whether you think senalties should be assigned to the property owner, or the
leaf blower operating company
With regard to the Reach codes under consideration for existing buildings, I would like to urge
members to support Reach Codes for cooling upgrades and planned home renovations for the
2025 building code cycle.  The new codes will ensure that alternatives to use of methane gas
are selected and reduce reliance on methane which is known to cause fires, contrlbute to
indoor air pollution, contribute to respiratory illnesses, and reduce carbon emissions that
contribute to climate change and worsen environmental justice.
Thank you for your consideration of these positive environmental, health and climate positive
improvements for our community.
Sincerely,

From:
Nancy
To:
Lisa Diaz Nash; Adam Loraine; Nicole Fernandez; City Mgr; City Attorney"s Office; Rob Newsom; Andrea Chow; Danielle Cwirko-Godycki
Subject:
Re; Leaf blowers ban: when convenient, can you kindly confirm receipt of this email? Thank you
Date:
Thursday, November 6, 2025 6:36:57 PM
Re; Leaf blowers ban: when convenient, can you kindly confirm receipt of this email? Thank you 
Nancy <
>
Oct 8, 2025, 9:39 PM
to CityCouncil, citymanager, achow, 
, Simona, cityattorneysoffice, 
, contact, rnewsom, alo
To : San Mateo City Council, City Manager, City Attorney, Staff (cc to interested parties such as Quiet Clean San Mateo and Sustainable San Mateo County)
If I understood the council comments at last Monday October 6, 2025 meeting re item# 17 leaf blowers, a couple of the council members seemed ready to extend leaf blower usage to Sundays. I completely disagree
with this. I think this is the time to think hard about why we need to allow the already extensive hours for leaf blowers --- whether electric or gas. I was shocked to hear that this would even be considered especially
when cities throughout the country are moving in the other direction i.e. to less noise.  It seemed rather tone deaf (no pun intended) to suggest this.
Many cities have not only banned gas leaf blowers, but also restrict hours of operation of electric leaf blowers much more than San Mateo does.  
Some cities outright ban all leaf blowers i.e. including electric.  Such cities include the following as reported by Sustainable San Mateo County: Leaf Blower Regulation
"Santa Monica, Manhattan Beach, Hermosa Beach, Laguna Beach and Del Mar ban the use of all leaf blowers, both gas and electric."
Many cities that do allow electric leaf blowers (but not gas), do not allow their commercial operation on Saturdays as well as Sundays.
Cities like Burlingame, have banned gas blowers and only allow electric blower usage on certain days of the week. 
Here are a couple of excerpts from Burlingame City site:
Leaf Blowers | Burlingame, CA
Gas Leaf Blower Ordinance
Gas leaf blowers are prohibited for use starting July 1, 2024 to reduce noise and air pollution in Burlingame.
Leaf Blowers | Burlingame, CA
Day of Use Restrictions
The City of Burlingame's Ordinance still restricts the number of days blowers may be used in each neighborhood:
Commercial blowers may be used on only one day per week, per area, with an extra day for R3 and R4 buildings.
Residents may use their own blowers on Saturdays from 9 am to 2 pm and Sundays from 10 am to 2 pm, as well as on their assigned weekday.
Leaf blower Operation Map
Leaf blower day of the week flyer in English, and Spanish
How did we get here?
At some point, the usage of leaf blowers "exploded" in the last 25 years ago. It became socially acceptable to allow these obnoxious machines to proliferate in our neighborhoods. As the wealth gap in areas like the
Bay Area increased, it became acceptable to farm out activities such as this to low-wage earners. (Similarly, the number of people in our city who have their bathrooms cleaned by low-wage earners, dramatically
increased.) Leaving aside the debate for now on the equity issues here, it is puzzling to me why we would so readily give up the peace in our neighborhoods (especially as more and more research shows how
important keeping noise pollution down is to the health of individuals, families, and communities).
There is a movement I read about called Make America Rake Again. Some cities like Mill Valley (which ban gas blowers), actually include such rake encouragement in their communications. 
Here is an excerpt from the City of Mill Valley site FAQs • Are gas-powered leaf blowers allowed in Mill Valley? regarding raking. It lists raking before using any motorized equipment.
"Instead of using gas-powered leaf blowers, residents and gardeners are encouraged to rake or sweep leaves and debris into piles and dispose of
them in the appropriate green trash bin. Residents and gardeners can also use electric leaf blowers or electric leaf vacuums to collect and
dispose of leaves. Please do not leave piles of leaves in the roadway. Leaves left in the street will block storm drains and may lead to flooding."
(By the way, I wonder if there is a certain fascination / obsession with motorized equipment/ tools in this country that does not exist in other countries. Recently I was in western Europe and surprised and delighted
to see how many accommodations have washers but  still no dryers.  They each had plastic folding simple racks that do the same job very well for the most part and with less energy of course.) :
In conclusion, I personally support the total ban of leaf blowers. For one, they are a noise nuisance that would not be tolerated in another form (e.g. a dog barking issue or neighbors yelling consistently).
If at this time, the council does not feel inclined to go this far, I would request serious consideration of policies that places like Burlingame have which assign one weekday for usage.
I would argue that a majority of residents would favor this. (By the way, time-permitting, I may start some informal surveying to see if my position is far off from others. I would send the results to the council, staff
and interested parties like Sustainable San Mateo County and Quiet Clean.)
P.S. I hope this is not offensive, but I would not advocate for keeping the policies on this issue "simple" at the expense of sound regulation. Honestly, it was not simple to research this issue and advocate for years
about it. We residents, all taxpayers in a democracy, should expect that things not be kept "simple" in lieu of our representation.
Thank you for your efforts and consideration,
Nancy Gogan
Addendum at 11.6.25
I did see there were many letters to the Council from other residents attached to the October 6 packet.  All expressed dislike of blowers.
An issue that I saw there, and really have not considered too much, is what is the actual content of the material that is being blown around --- whether by gas or electric blowers. It is a little disturbing  I think to
contemplate the variety of pollutants, including from animal waste, that is distributed from these machines.

all leaf blowers, both gas and electric."

certain days of the week.

Here are a couple of excerpts from Burlingame City site:

Leaf Blowers | Burlingame, CA

Gas Leaf Blower Ordinance

Gas leaf blowers are prohibited for use starting July 1, 2024 to reduce noise and air
pollution in Burlingame.

Leaf Blowers | Burlingame, CA
Day of Use Restrictions

The City of Burlingame's Ordinance still restricts the number of days blowers may be
used in each neighborhood:

e Commercial blowers may be used on only one day per week, per area, with an
extra day for R3 and R4 buildings.

Residents may use their own blowers on Saturdays from 9 am to 2 pm and
Sundays from 10 am to 2 pm, as well as on their assigned weekday.

e Leaf blower Operation Map
e Leaf blower day of the week flyer in English, and Spanish

603 of 662


--- page 604 ---
How did we get here?
At some point, the usage of leaf blowers "exploded" in the last 25 years ago. It became
socially acceptable to allow these obnoxious machines to proliferate in our neighborhoods. As
the wealth gap in areas like the Bay Area increased, it became acceptable to farm out
activities such as this to low-wage earners. (Similarly, the number of people in our city who
have their bathrooms cleaned by low-wage earners, dramatically increased.) Leaving aside the
debate for now on the equity issues here, it is puzzling to me why we would so readily give up
the peace in our neighborhoods (especially as more and more research shows how important
keeping noise pollution down is to the health of individuals, families, and communities).
There is a movement I read about called Make America Rake Again. Some cities like Mill
Valley (which ban gas blowers), actually include such rake encouragement in their
communications. 
Here is an excerpt from the City of Mill Valley site FAQs • Are gas-powered leaf blowers
allowed in Mill Valley? regarding raking. It lists raking before using any motorized
equipment.
"Instead of using gas-powered leaf blowers, residents and gardeners are
encouraged to rake or sweep leaves and debris into piles and dispose of them
in the appropriate green trash bin. Residents and gardeners can also use
electric leaf blowers or electric leaf vacuums to collect and dispose of leaves.
Please do not leave piles of leaves in the roadway. Leaves left in the street will
block storm drains and may lead to flooding."
(By the way, I wonder if there is a certain fascination / obsession with motorized equipment/
tools in this country that does not exist in other countries. Recently I was in western Europe
and surprised and delighted to see how many accommodations have washers but  still no
dryers.  They each had plastic folding simple racks that do the same job very well for the most
part and with less energy of course.) :
In conclusion, I personally support the total ban of leaf blowers. For one, they are a noise
nuisance that would not be tolerated in another form (e.g. a dog barking issue or neighbors
yelling consistently).
If at this time, the council does not feel inclined to go this far, I would request serious
consideration of policies that places like Burlingame have which assign one weekday for
usage.
I would argue that a majority of residents would favor this. (By the way, time-permitting, I
may start some informal surveying to see if my position is far off from others. I would send
the results to the council, staff and interested parties like Sustainable San Mateo County and
Quiet Clean.)
P.S. I hope this is not offensive, but I would not advocate for keeping the policies on this issue
"simple" at the expense of sound regulation. Honestly, it was not simple to research this issue
and advocate for years about it. We residents, all taxpayers in a democracy, should expect that
things not be kept "simple" in lieu of our representation.
Thank you for your efforts and consideration,
Nancy Gogan

On Tue, Oct 7, 2025 at 2:22 PM Mark Miller

Dear Mayor Newsom, San Mateo City Council and San Mateo City Clerk,
 
Thank you for taking the time to review my letter regarding the consideration of a gas leaf
blower ban for the City of San Mateo. I appreciate your ongoing commitment to protecting
both the health and well-being of our residents and our environment.
607 of 662

--- page 608 ---
I understand that there are a variety of factors to consider when implementing a new policy,
especially regarding the operational hours for electric leaf blowers in residential areas.
However, after careful thought, I would like to address one specific concern that has been
raised regarding expanding the operational hours to include Sundays which was discussed
at the City Council Regular Meeting on Monday, October 6, 2025.
I strongly believe that adding Sunday to the operational hours would create an unnecessary
disturbance to the quiet, peaceful environment that is so vital for the well-being of San
Mateo’s residents. Sundays, in particular, serve as an important day for relaxation,
reflection, and family time. It is a day when many of us are home and deserve the opportunity
to enjoy a quieter, more serene environment.
Even the noise generated by electric powered leaf blowers can be disruptive and detrimental
to the quality of life for residents, many of whom work long hours during the week. Limiting
their use to Monday - Saturday would strike a better balance -- allowing the necessary yard
work to be done while also respecting the need for a peaceful environment.
I respectfully urge the Council to maintain the current proposal, which restricts leaf blower
use to Monday - Saturday for residential areas, and to avoid expanding the operational
hours to include Sundays. This compromise would achieve the desired environmental
benefits, while also protecting the tranquility that so many of us cherish.
Once again, thank you for your leadership in protecting our environment and the health of our
community. I trust that the City Council will continue to consider both the environmental
impact and the quality of life for San Mateo residents as you move forward with this
important decision.
 
I appreciate your attention and your consideration of this request.
-- Mark Miller

From: Dustin Chase 
 
Sent: Wednesday, October 22, 2025 9:02 AM

 City Attorney's Office

<clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>
Subject: Decision Framework - Electric Leaf Blower Transition
 
Honorable Council, 
Quiet Clean San Mateo’s goal is to bring the best of the best ideas to the gas leaf blower prohibition
program in San Mateo. We appreciate the feedback and comments presented during the October 6
study session. 
Finalizing the electric leaf blower prohibition program can be challenging. Please find attached a
decision framework we hope will ease the process. 
Thank you,
Dustin
611 of 662

--- page 612 ---
Decision Framework 
 
The San Mateo City Council’s October 6 gas leaf blower prohibition study session raised many 
questions regarding various ordinance details. It appeared difficult to resolve some of the policy 
questions.  
 
In response, Quiet Clean San Mateo is offering a decision framework that will hopefully ease the 
burden on decision makers. This seeks to restate the goals and principles we observe in San 
Mateo government, and illustrate the status quo in San Mateo regarding gas leaf blowers.  
 
Our Goals and Guiding Principles 
 
Our Goal 
 
Our goal is to foster community vibrancy.  The means to achieve our goal is reducing or 
eliminating machine interference in community activities.  
 
I live in the Mission District of San Francisco. I experience many amazing community activities, 
such as impromptu street dance parties. Sometimes those things are louder than I’d like, but 
these activities are about people expressing themselves and living joyfully. It’s people making 
my community more vibrant.  
 
In San Mateo, we prioritized closing some streets to cars downtown and reducing train noise. 
Both those actions reduced machine activity, so people can enjoy a better quality of life in San 
Mateo.  
 
Guiding Principles 
 
People increase community vibrancy. Machines dull it. We’re eliminating a particularly nasty 
machine from our community.  
 
Simplicity. We want to minimize city workload and set clear expectations for landscaping 
businesses, property owners and the entire community. We will have one set of rules for 
everyone to follow, and simple procedures for accepting and resolving complaints.  
 
Decision Factors  
 
The Impact 
 
The low frequency noise a gas leaf blower produces is extremely loud, carries over 2 football 
fields away, has little drop off over that distance, and penetrates physical barriers such as doors, 
windows, and even walls. This noise causes hypertension, stress and aggression.  
 
612 of 662

--- page 613 ---
Every single gas leaf blower violation matters because even a single gas leaf blower can 
severely impact hundreds of homes. 
 
For those of us who are not exposed to this noise, it is all too easy to dismiss it. But it’s 
undeniable physics. Noise is vibration, and gas leaf blower noise powerfully affects the human 
nervous system. It is undeniable physics vs fragile human beings.  
 
It’s no contest: physics always wins. That is why we see people like the resident who asked at 
the October 6 study session for an immediate gas leaf blower ban. He seems to be struggling 
much the same way I was in San Mateo, and I hate to think he might end up making an 
emergency escape from San Mateo as I was forced to.  
 
There is high tension and much conflict in San Mateo neighborhoods over gas leaf blowers. We 
will relieve that tension by both prohibiting gas leaf blower use in San Mateo, and effectively 
enforcing that prohibition.  
 
Electric Compromise 
 
Leaf blowers are not necessary for plant care. They are a convenience for property owners, and 
they’ve become the de facto tool for landscapers.  
 
Banning leaf blowers altogether would best serve our goal of fostering community vibrancy. But 
property owners like the convenience of leaf blowers, and landscaping businesses insisted they 
need them, so we compromised on electric models.  
 
These are substantially quieter and produce higher frequency noise than gas leaf blowers do. 
So, electric leaf blower noise does not travel nearly as far as gas leaf blower noise does, and it 
is more easily blocked. But many electric leaf blower models are still quite loud, and many 
people find them disturbing. My experience with electric leaf blower noise has varied, with some 
electric models being easily tolerable and others setting my nerves on edge. I could never adapt 
to gas leaf blower noise, but I can to electric ones. It’s a compromise.  
 
Electric leaf blowers have some of the same drawbacks gas models do because they do exactly 
the same thing: blow. They can still kick up pollen, dust and animal feces. Those with asthma 
and other respiratory illnesses will still be affected by electric leaf blowers. However, 
successfully transitioning to electric leaf blowers requires businesses to use them much more 
efficiently than the old gas models. The resulting lower speeds should mitigate the huge clouds 
of dust we see around gas leaf blower operators. Again, this is a compromise with landscaping 
business owners and their customers in the name of harm reduction.  
 
In this compromise, landscaping business owners got what they asked for and more than they 
knew. They not only got to keep using leaf blowers, but also got safer working conditions and 
higher profit margins. They also get financial assistance with the upfront investment in superior 
tools.  
 
613 of 662

--- page 614 ---
Their workers’ safety is the main driver behind this change. So, the individuals who are so often 
thought to be most harmed by this prohibition are those who benefit most.  
 
Our biggest gain with this transition is the elimination of local gas leaf blower emissions. The 
community will still have to contend with leaf blower noise and dust, but at much lower levels 
with electric models than with the old gas ones. In exchange for tolerating electric leaf blowers, 
they get significantly cleaner air, safer neighborhoods and a more vibrant San Mateo.  
 
The XY Problem 
 
Some landscaping business owners have some concerns about electric leaf blowers. Let’s not 
address these one by one. Instead, let’s address their source.  
 
Many landscaping businesses are trapped in what is called an “XY Problem” with leaf blowers. 
It’s a common problem amongst human beings. It’s when we discuss the end user's attempted 
solution (X) rather than the root problem itself (Y). 
 
Some landscaping businesses see themselves as a leaf blower service, not a landscaping 
service. They train and supervise their employees as leaf blower operators, not as landscaping 
workers. The following examples are given not to demonize anyone, but to show how the XY 
problem shows up in landscaping services, so we can make better policy decisions.  
1.​ A landscaping business employee is directed to spend 20 minutes at the customer’s 
property. There is very little debris to clear, but the employee has no choice but to spend 
the full 20 minutes running their leaf blower at virtually nothing. (San Mateo public 
comment)​
 
2.​ A tenant wants the landscaper to, at least, spend less time running the gas leaf blower. 
He times the landscaper, then times himself doing the same job with a push broom. They 
are about the same. Another day, he meticulously clears the entire property with a push 
broom shortly before the landscaper arrives. The landscaper spends no less time using 
the gas leaf blower than he usually does, blowing the entire, practically pristine property. 
(My observation)​
 
3.​ Using a gas leaf blower at full throttle, a landscaper scatters leaves and debris around 
the customer’s property. He clears one area, does another, then goes to the previous 
area to clear the debris he just blew back into the area he just cleared. Back and forth the 
worker goes, eventually herding all the debris into a single pile in the street. He then 
blows the pile around and up the street to make it appear that he removed them. (My 
observation)​
 
4.​ A leaf blower operator finds a few leaves and trimmings in an apartment courtyard. 
Instead of sweeping up the small pile, he blows it all the way around the courtyard and 
into the street. (Reddit)​
 
614 of 662

--- page 615 ---
5.​ A landscaper blows an entire parking lot, whether there was any debris in a particular 
area or not. As he works, he produces a huge cloud of fine dust, which settle\s over the 
parking lot. He then blows every inch of the parking lot, again, to remove that dust. (My 
observation) 
The  numerous landscaping businesses that work efficiently and use leaf blowers appropriately 
are not likely to complain about the transition to electric leaf blowers. We’re more likely to hear 
complaints from businesses that use leaf blowers as much as possible, at the highest power 
possible. The XY problem is the source of battery life and tool performance complaints.  
 
These landscaping business owners themselves are stuck in legacy practices, with gas leaf 
blowers the ultimate problem. They invite abuse. Convenient gas/oil refilling means you can get 
away with wasting fuel, so you use full power all the time. You can also use the blower for every 
speck of debris, no matter how small. The hurricane-force wind moves so much debris at once 
that you are fooled into believing you are working quickly. Really, that tool is wasting your time 
and money.  
 
Moving on from gas leaf blowers means solving the XY Problem. Don’t ask “how can I use my 
leaf blower?” but “how can I best get my work done?” The City of San Mateo did exactly that 
when it replaced much of its leaf blowing with a Brush Hog.  
 
Customer Expectations 
 
Landscaping business owners react to customer demands, which can lead to excessive, 
wasteful leaf blower use. Some property owners expect their outdoor areas to be pristine. One 
of my old San Mateo neighbors used her leaf blower multiple times each day in pursuit of that 
goal. 
 
Summary 
 
We are eliminating gas leaf blowers in service to community vibrancy. The challenge to this 
change is mainly in customer expectations and legacy landscaping practices. We will address 
these challenges simply and effectively.  
 
615 of 662

Subject:
3/2/2026 agenda item #7 public comment on leaf blower ordinance
Dear San Mateo City Council: 
 
To ensure success, an ordinance must be strong and enforced, and 
its purpose must be understood by the community.  The purpose of 
the leaf blower ordinance is not to address complaints, it is to 
protect the health and safety of our community.  This point can 
be clarified in the PURPOSE section 10.80.010 of the municipal 
code. 
 
Upon receipt of code violation reports during the 9 month period 
leading up to the enforcement date of January 1, 2027, city staff 
are encouraged to send courtesy notices to property owners and 
businesses.  This will enable a quicker and more successful transition 
to cleaner tools, and should ease the enforcement burden on the 
code 
enforcement department once strict enforcement begins.  Portions of 
this process can be automated. 
 
A successful ordinance must be equitable. 
Please adopt the ordinance city-wide with no exceptions or exemptions, 
and add Juneteenth National Independence Day to the list of federal 
holidays on which all leaf blower operation is prohibited. 
 
Please consider adding the language: 
"No person shall operate or authorize the use of an internal 
combustion generator to charge batteries for, or, provide power to, 
leaf blowers." 
 
Please incorporate language in the ordinance now that will prohibit the 
use of gas-powered string and hedge trimmers starting January 1, 2028. 
Don't leave this task to a future city council! 
 
And last but not least, allow us members of the community to aid in this 
616 of 662

--- page 617 ---
2
healing transition to better health for workers, businesses, residents, 
and visitors in our city. 
 
Thank you for doing the right thing – protecting our health, safety, 
and quality of life. 
 
Sincerely, 
 
Carol Wilhelmy

Sal Outlook

We support leaf blower ban.

From Dave Clark
Date Mon 3/2/2026 8:00 PM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Wow, the city council is doing great. Can’t make it to the end of the meeting for comments.
I’ve been corresponding with the city about LB Ban for perhaps seven years.

Leaf Blower Issues — the ornithologists are chiming in.
In addition to C02, Air Turbidity (dust), Worker Health (noise, fumes, physical), destruction (hardening)
of soil, and neighborhood noise there is now strong evidence that they affect other critters.

Leaf blowers severely disrupt bird mating by creating high-intensity noise and environmental disruption,
acting as a "sensory pollutant". This noise masks mating calls, hinders territorial defense, and causes
chronic stress, reducing reproductive success. The physical disturbance also destroys foraging habitats,
kills food sources (insects/spiders), and degrades nesting sites.

Leaf Blower Costs

| provided some draft costing figures to Andrea. | ran a small business for 30 years and learned to bend
and weave with new laws and customer demands. | calculated a net cost to the landscaper of less than
$2.50 / month per house (ballpark). In the age of $19 burritos, we just learn to live with higher costs.
Thanks for following through with this !

Dave Clark
San Mateo

FROM:

MEETING DATE:
March 02, 2026 
SUBJECT:
Boards and Commissions — Establishment of Annual Appointment Subcommittees and Vacancy Process
RECOMMENDATION:
Establish the Appointment Subcommittees for the 2026 annual Boards and Commissions recruitment cycle; provide 
direction to staff regarding any Council Members who wish to serve on additional Appointment Subcommittees, whether 
eligible Commissioners may be automatically reappointed to an additional term, which upcoming vacancies will require 
incumbents to reapply and reinterview, and any additional direction necessary to carry out the 2026 recruitment process.
BACKGROUND:
City Council’s Rules and Procedures provide direction for the selection and appointment process for Boards and 
Commission members. The City Clerk’s Office administers this process, initially by notifying commissioners with terms that 
are scheduled to expire, verifying interest in continuing to serve, and coordination of the necessary recruitment, including 
outreach and engagement with the community. 
UPCOMING VACANCIES 
For the 2026 annual Boards and Commissions recruitment cycle, the City will have 11 commissioner positions and 7 Youth 
Commissioner positions with terms set to expire. Two commissioners—Board Member Murphy and Commissioner 
Robbins—have completed two consecutive four-year terms on their respective advisory bodies and, pursuant to Section 
6.05 of the City Charter, are not eligible for reappointment. In addition, Ellie Chiu, the Youth Commissioner to the Senior 
Advisory Commission, will be ineligible to serve an additional term as she will turn 18 prior to the expiration of her current 
term.
The Civic Arts Commission was established by Ordinance 2025-10; therefore, all five seats will be open for appointment 
during this recruitment cycle. The duration of term for each appointee will be determined by lot at the Commission’s first 
regularly scheduled meeting. Staff will also be recruiting Youth Commissioners for first time for this Commission.
The City’s Rules and Procedures provide that the default process is for currently serving commissioners to participate in an 
interview for reappointment. Alternatively, the Council may approve an automatic reappointment by majority vote based 
on outstanding performance or other factors warranting continuity in the role. In 2025, the City Council directed that all 
commissioners interested in continuing their service reapply and participate in the interview process. 
Table 1 below identifies the commissioners with expiring terms, the length of their service on the commission, and their 
expressed interest in continued service.

There are some factors the Council may wish to consider regarding appropriateness for automatic reappointments or 
requiring incumbents to reapply and reinterview. The 2025 spring recruitment cycle resulted in a 78% increase in 
applications compared to the prior year, contributing to a more competitive selection process and expanded community 
awareness of the role and responsibilities of commissioners. Following the recruitment cycle, staff received feedback from 
some incumbents, including those appointed to partial terms, regarding the requirement to complete a reapplication and 
interview process within a relatively short timeframe after their initial appointment. The City Clerk’s Office also monitors 
commissioner attendance at meetings. For the incumbents referenced above, attendance has been consistent with Council 
expectations. Council may wish to note that one Youth Commissioner seeking reappointment to the Library Board has had 
limited attendance during the current term.
Council may wish to balance the goal of expanding community participation through open recruitment with considerations 
of continuity, institutional knowledge, and fairness to commissioners appointed to partial terms.
Question 1. 
Does the Council wish to authorize automatic reappointment for any eligible incumbents, or require all 
interested incumbents to reapply and participate in the interview process?
APPOINTMENT SUBCOMMITTEE ESTABLISHMENT
As part of this discussion, Council will be asked to establish Appointment Subcommittees to serve on interview panels and 
provide recommendations for appointment to the full Council for any scheduled or unscheduled vacancies that occur 
throughout the year. The City’s Rules and Procedures provide that, at a minimum, each Appointment Subcommittee shall 
include the Mayor (or the Mayor’s designee) and the Council liaison to the respective commission. Table 2 below identifies 
the default Appointment Subcommittee assignments.
The Rules and Procedures further provide that an Appointment Subcommittee may include up to five Council Members. 
During the 2025 recruitment cycle, a majority (three or more) of the City Council participated on all Appointment 
Subcommittees for commission vacancies. When a majority of the Council serves on an Appointment Subcommittee, the 
interviews constitute a Special City Council meeting, which requires additional noticing and administrative coordination by 
627 of 662

--- page 628 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
staff. Historically, the use of smaller Appointment Subcommittees was intended to provide scheduling flexibility and to 
avoid the need to convene additional full Council meetings solely for the purpose of conducting interviews.
Table 2: Default Appointment Subcommittees for 2026
Board, Commission, or Committee
Default Appointment Subcommittee

Mayor Loraine and Council Member Diaz Nash
Community Relations Commission
Mayor Loraine and Deputy Mayor Fernandez
Library Board
Mayor Loraine and Council Member Diaz Nash

Mosquito & Vector Control District
Mayor Loraine and Deputy Mayor Fernandez
Question 2. 
Does the Council wish to confirm the default Appointment Subcommittees as identified in Table 2, or 
modify assignments and/or expand participation?
2026 OUTREACH AND RECRUITMENT SUCCESS PLAN:
The annual Boards and Commissions recruitment process serves as a critical mechanism for advancing civic participation, 
strengthening community representation, and supporting the City Council’s policy priorities. 
In early 2025, the City Clerk’s Office presented the results of the 2025 Demography Study to the City Council. The survey 
identified continued disparities between the demographic composition of the broader San Mateo community and 
representation on the City’s Boards, Commissions, and Committees. Following this presentation, the City Council provided 
direction to staff to explore additional strategies to strengthen outreach and recruitment efforts.
In response, and informed by further research into best practices among city clerk’s offices throughout the State, as well as 
alignment with the City’s Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Strategic Framework, staff refined and expanded the City’s 
outreach strategy. The City Clerk’s Office has since updated the 2026 Outreach and Recruitment Success Plan (Attachment 
1) to incorporate Council feedback, including the continued use of digital recruitment advertisements, and include 
measurable performance metrics.
Digital recruitment advertisements were launched for the first time during the 2025 recruitment cycle and will be utilized 
again in 2026. In 2025, the campaign generated 251,992 display impressions, significantly expanding the City’s reach 
beyond traditional communication channels. The 2025 campaign contributed to the significant increase in total 
applications received during the recruitment period. 
Question 3. 
Does the Council have additional direction regarding outreach strategies, recruitment methods, or 
performance metrics for the 2026 recruitment cycle?

There is no budgetary impact associated with this administrative action.

direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)

2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Broaden the outreach and recruitment strategies for Boards, Commissions, 
and Committees to bolster inclusive participation reflecting the diversity of the 
City of San Mateo’s community.  
 
 
This plan aims to establish priorities, strategies, and messaging for Boards, 
Commissions, and Committee’s recruitment outreach in 2026 and beyond 
within the City Clerk’s Office. Aligned with the Clerk’s Office Diversity, Equity, 
and Inclusion Strategic Framework Initiative, its objective is to enhance 
communication and engagement during the recruitment process effectively, 
thereby improving overall effectiveness and wider community participation. 
 
 
• Increase the number of applicants per vacancy. 
• Broaden the range of applicants by engaging with individuals from 
underrepresented groups. 
• Utilize established partnerships and explore new collaborations to 
advertise and highlight vacancies. 
• Inform the community about the City's advisory committees and ways 
for individuals to engage and take part in these opportunities. 
 
 
 
• Conduct and analyze results from regularly scheduled demographic 
studies of Boards, Commissions, and Committees, including applicant 
data. 
• Track levels of engagement on recruitment efforts through social media 
and associated webpages.  
City Clerk’s Office Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion  
Strategic Framework Initiative 
Outreach Plan and Purpose 
Objectives 
Metrics for Success 
Overview 
634 of 662

(See March 3, 2025 Boards and Commissions – 2025 Demography Study 
Agenda Report for a full comparative analysis). 
 
While the 2025 Boards and Commissions Demographic Survey revealed 
progress and positive trends, representation disparities were identified.  
Several populations are underrepresented. Compared to the city’s general 
population, people of color, multilingual individuals, renters, and young adults 
are underrepresented on our Boards & Commissions. The study also revealed 
geographic disparities in representation between Council districts. 
 
During this year’s recruitment cycle, a concentrated effort will be made to 
reach these underrepresented populations.   
 
 
 
• Identify opportunities to leverage existing relationships with community 
organizations and faith-based institutions.  
 
• Partner directly with organization leadership to promote vacancies at 
regularly scheduled meetings and special events.   
 
• Expand existing contact list and further develop connections that have 
been established but are potentially underutilized.   
 
• Consider smaller groups comprised of volunteers, event organizers, and 
neighborhood leaders.

Overarching priorities to bolster recruitment and outreach to members of 
underrepresented communities within the City of San Mateo: 
 
Priority #1: Engage with Community Members & Partner to Bolster Outreach Efforts 
Work directly with partners to engage, collaborate, and communicate throughout 
the outreach & recruitment process. 
 
Priority #2: Accurate, Open, and Strategic Communication Efforts  
Promptly distribute accurate information related to B&C recruitment through a 
variety of City communication channels. Strategize key messages and create high-
quality content to effectively reach and educate potential applicants.  
 
Priority #3: Data Collection 
Monitor progress and collect data throughout this year’s outreach and recruitment 
cycle.

Outreach to New Residents 
According to the 2025 survey, the average number of years lived in San Mateo 
was 24.5 years, with over 77% residing in San Mateo for over a decade.  
 
A longstanding connection to the city continues to be a desirable attribute 
for applicants, however, outreach to new community members provides 
opportunities to bolster participation in future recruitments and attract 
applicants with different lived perspectives and experiences. 
 
Outreach Objectives:  
• Share Boards & Commissions informational material with organizations 
that provide realty and rental services to new residents.  
• Include Boards & Commissions informational material in new resident 
resources.  
 
Expand existing contact list and build relationships with community 
organizations whose member-base consists of individuals who identify as 
Hispanic and Latino, as well as those who identify as Asian. 
According to the 2025 B&C Demography Survey, none of the survey 
respondents identified as Hispanic, Latino, or of Spanish origin. 24% of the San 
Mateo population shares this identification. Representation of respondents 
who identify as Asian was 14% in 2025. 26% of the San Mateo population 
identifies as Asian. 
 
Outreach Objectives: 
• Identify opportunities to leverage existing relationships with 
community organizations and faith-based institutions. 
o Partner directly with organization leadership to promote 
vacancies at regularly scheduled meetings and special events.  
• Expand existing contact list and further develop connections that have 
been established but are potentially underutilized.  
o Consider smaller groups comprised of volunteers, event 
organizers, and neighborhood leaders. 
 
 
Survey Findings and Recommendations for the 2025 Boards & 
Commissions Recruitment Cycle 
637 of 662

--- page 638 ---
6 
 
Expand Messaging to Multilingual Applicants 
A smaller proportion of commissioners speak a language other than English 
at home compared to the general population. B&C members are largely 
monolingual English speakers. Roughly half of San Mateo’s population speaks 
a language other than English.  
 
At present, representation on B&Cs for Asian and Pacific Islander and Spanish 
speaking communities is minimal compared to the San Mateo population. 
Note, that while the city is not exploring recruitment specific to members of 
the community whose primary language is not English, outreach to 
multilingual individuals broadens the range of input available to advisory 
members as well as members of the community who speak a language other 
than English and may have limited English proficiency.  Incorporating 
outreach to multilingual communities and organizations will serve to 
increase participation, engagement and representation from community 
members who do not speak English as their primary language.    
 
Outreach Objectives: 
• Conduct outreach to applicants who speak a second language to 
narrow the divide between local government and community 
members who have limited English language proficiency. 
• Provide translated resources. 
 
Youth-Targeted Educational Messaging 
Age distribution amongst Boards and Commissioners is closely aligned, 
consistently representative of the 25-44 age bracket, but over-represent the 
65+ age bracket. However, San Mateo B&Cs lack young adult representation. 
In 2025, there were no young adults between the age of 18 and 24. Youth 
representation has increased overall, with the introduction of Youth 
Commissioner positions on the Park and Recreation Commission, 
Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, Senior Advisory Commission, 
and Library Board.  
 
Outreach Objectives: 
• Develop material to educate younger populations and introduce them 
to the idea of serving on a Board or Commission (if they are not 
presently able to apply or ineligible due to age, they may participate in 
future recruitments). 
• Share educational material with high school student leadership 
programs and voter education programs to encourage youth 
engagement.

Outreach to Educational Institutions  
According to the 2025 survey, San Mateo’s Boards and Commissions 
members are highly educated, with about 66.7% holding a post-graduate 
degree in 2025, which represents a decrease from 75.6% in 2023. 33.3% of 
Commissioners in 2025 hold a bachelor’s or associate degree, representing an 
increase from 24.4% in 2023.   
 
An educated applicant pool is greatly desired. However, individuals who do 
not yet possess but are actively pursuing higher education (or participating in 
alternative professional programs) may also be potentially viable candidates. 
Additionally, those pursuing advanced degrees or certifications are generally 
comprised of young adults, which is also a target population.  
 
Outreach Objectives: 
• Conduct outreach to local educational institutions and professional 
programs. 
 
Outreach to renters, apartment communities, and nonprofit rental 
housing providers.  
Renters remain under-represented. The results who that 10.8% of boards and 
commissioners rent or live with parents. 4.4% are identified as other living 
arrangements. There continues to be an over-representation of homeowners 
in 2025. 
 
Outreach Objectives 
• Contact apartment communities and local tenant organizations.

a decrease of almost 10 percentage points from 2023. District 2 remains 
underrepresented at 8% and experienced a decrease of 2.42 percentage 
points. Districts 3, 4, and 5 experienced increases in representation. 
 
Outreach Objectives: 
• Conduct outreach to neighborhood associations/groups & 
organizations in those specific areas. 
• Partner with Council Members representing these districts to reach 
members of these communities.

Purpose of This Discussion
Establish the 2026 Appointment Subcommittees and provide direction on upcoming term 
expirations and the recruitment process.
Question 1. 
Does the Council wish to authorize automatic 
reappointment for any eligible incumbents, or 
require all interested incumbents to reapply and 
participate in the interview process?

--- page 651 ---
Reappointment Process – Policy Considerations
Default Process (Rules & Procedures)
• Incumbent commissioners participate in an interview for reappointment.
• Council may approve automatic reappointment by majority vote based on 
outstanding performance or continuity considerations.
• In 2025, Council required all interested incumbents to reapply and interview.
Context for 2026
• 2025 spring recruitment saw a 78% increase in applications, expanding community 
awareness and competition.
• Feedback received from some incumbents (including partial-term appointees) 
regarding reapplying within a short timeframe of appointment.
• Attendance for incumbents referenced above has been consistent with Council 
expectations.

--- page 654 ---
Appointment Subcommittee Establishment
• Appointment Subcommittees:
• Serve on interview panels
• Review applicants
• Provide appointment recommendations to the full Council
• Address scheduled and unscheduled vacancies throughout the year
Rules & Procedures Requirements:
• Each Appointment Subcommittee shall include:
• The Mayor (or Mayor’s designee)
• The Council liaison to the respective commission
• Subcommittees may include up to five Council Members
654 of 662

--- page 655 ---
Appointment Subcommittee Establishment
2025 Practice
• A majority (3+ Council Members) participated on all Appointment 
Subcommittees
• When a majority serves:
• Interviews constitute a Special City Council Meeting
• Additional noticing and staff coordination are required
Historical Practice
• Smaller subcommittees were intended to:
• Provide scheduling flexibility
• Avoid convening additional full Council meetings solely for interviews

--- page 657 ---
Boards & Commissions Recruitment Overview
Expanded Outreach Strategy
In response to Council direction, staff:
• Conducted research into best practices among city clerk offices 
statewide
• Aligned recruitment efforts with the City’s Diversity, Equity, and 
Inclusion Strategic Framework
• Refined and expanded outreach methods
• Next demography study scheduled for 2027
The 2026 Outreach & Recruitment Success Plan:
• Incorporate Council feedback
• Continue digital recruitment advertisements
• Include measurable performance metrics
